title: About
text:
I am a member of the Lipid Lab and we are currently investigating the mechanisms of lipid droplet synthesis and degradation in Tetrahymena thermophila! I am also an internationally certified Supplemental Instruction leader for introductory chemistry courses, namely CHEM 122: Introductory Chemistry and CHEM 126: Energies and Rates of Chemical Reaction.










Me in the lab!

title: Cynthia Budiyanto
text: Hi everyone!  My name is Cynthia Budiyanto and I am an international student from Indonesia at St. Olaf College. I am majoring in Biology and Statistics & Data Science, with a concentration in Biomolecular Science. In my college, I am actively involved in research and student teaching. I am passionate about scientific development and I would love to do research that is application-driven, aiming to produce computational models that integrate diverse data sources to better understand and treat diseases. Welcome to my website!

title: MP1_DataWrangling
text:
Loading data set…

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(lubridate)
library(tidytext)
library(textdata)

lipid_old <- read_csv("~/SDS264_S26/Project/MP1/abstracts.csv") # with Miscellaneous column

Warning: One or more parsing issues, call `problems()` on your data frame for details,
e.g.:
  dat <- vroom(...)
  problems(dat)


Rows: 9754 Columns: 7
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (7): Journal, Title, Authors, Author_Information, Abstract, DOI, Misc

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

lipid <- read_csv("~/SDS264_S26/Project/MP1/abstracts_lipid.csv") # without Miscellaneous column

Warning: One or more parsing issues, call `problems()` on your data frame for details,
e.g.:
  dat <- vroom(...)
  problems(dat)


Rows: 9752 Columns: 6
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (6): Journal, Title, Authors, Author_Information, Abstract, DOI

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.




Data wrangling
Note: Purpose of the code chunks is given prior to the code lines.
It came to my observation that there are 112 articles in which the “authors” column are shifted to the right, because the articles are written in other languages, and now the “authors” column contains information regarding what language the article was originally written in. The next column, which is the “authors information” column, contain the actual list of authors. The “abstract” column, then contains author information/origin, and so on. So we are going to fix that. There is also a row that does not have author information at all. We are going to remove that as well.

multilanguage_fixed <- lipid_old |>
  # First of all, we are removing rows without any information regarding the authors.
  filter(Authors != "[No authors listed]") |>
  # Second, we are interested specifically to fix the problem I diagnosed above for articles written in languages beside English. Their authors column has an identical pattern that we can filter. 
  filter(str_detect(Authors, "^\\[")) |>
  # We do not want this column anymore because it is irrelevant.
  select(!Authors) |>
  # Shifting the columns to the left and renaming them properly. So we have correct information in the correct column.
  mutate(authors = Author_Information,
         author_information = Abstract,
         abstract = DOI,
         DOI = Misc) |>
  rename_with(tolower, .cols = c(Journal, Title, DOI)) |>
  select(journal, title, authors, author_information, abstract, doi) |>
  # In the code chunk below, I am removing some noticeable miscellaneous problems and troublesome rows that will make later data processing easier.
  mutate(title = str_remove_all(title, "\\[|\\]")) |>
  filter(!is.na(doi)) |>
  filter(str_detect(author_information, "^A"))

Now that we have fixed that portion of the data frame, I will rejoin them with the rest of the data, and also fix miscellaneous formatting style from that lipid data frame.

lipid_new <- lipid |>
  # Renaming them to match the name convention I made above.
  rename_with(tolower, .cols = where(is.character)) |>
  # Removing the articles not written in English
  filter(!str_detect(authors, "^\\[")) |>
  # Removing retracted article without proper title and full information
  filter(!str_detect(journal, "RETRACTED ARTICLE")) |>
  # Selecting only rows with proper author information
  filter(str_detect(author_information, "^A")) |>
  # Removing rows without proper abstract information
  filter(!str_detect(abstract, "^Erratum")) |>
  filter(!str_detect(abstract, regex("doi", ignore_case = TRUE))) |>
  # Joining the dataset with the fixed dataset for articles not written in English.
  # Would be much less work if I just filter them out without doing all of the fixes, but the article collections would not be as diverse, because then it would only include articles from mainly Western countries. 
  full_join(multilanguage_fixed)

Joining with `by = join_by(journal, title, authors, author_information,
abstract, doi)`


Now that we have a cleaner dataset, I feel more comfortable with using more stringr functions for mutating.

lipidata <- lipid_new |>
  mutate(
    # Extracting only the abbreviated journal name, from the full citation format "journal" column.
    j_name = str_extract(journal, "^\\d*\\. [A-Za-z].*?\\."),
    j_name = str_replace(j_name, "^\\d*\\.", ""),
    # Extracting only the published date, from the full citation format "journal" column. Though this is not parsed into date and is still in character vectors. 
    date_published = str_extract(journal, " \\d{4}.*\\d*;| \\d{4}.*\\d*\\.| \\d{4}.*\\d*:"),
    date_published = str_replace(date_published, "(;|:|\\.).*", ""),
    # Removing the redundant element from author information column.
    author_information = str_remove(author_information, "Author information:"),
    # Extracting the year, month, and date, therefore it would be easier to play with if I need it. I do this because a lot of them only have incomplete dates. 
    year = str_extract(date_published, "\\d{4}"),
    month = str_extract(date_published, "[A-Za-z]{3}"),
    day = str_extract(date_published, " \\d{1,2}$"),
    # Counting the number of authors for each scientific article. Might be something interesting that I would like to know.
    num_authors = str_count(authors, ","),
    num_authors = num_authors + 1,
    # Extracting the First Author and their main institution foe each scientific article.
    FA = str_extract(authors, "^[A-Z](\\w|-)* [A-Za-z]{1,}"),
    FA_institution = str_remove(str_extract(author_information, "\\(1\\)[^\\(]*"), "\\(1\\)"),
    # Counting the number of words and characters for each abstract. 
    abstract_words = str_count(abstract, "([^ ]+)"),
    abstract_chr = str_length(abstract),
    # Counting the number of words and characters for each title. 
    title_words = str_count(title, "([^ ]+)"),
    title_length = str_length(title)) |>
  # We do not really need this actually, so I would just exclude them from being incorporated into the cleaner version of the data frame. 
  select(!journal, !doi)



New data frame after wrangling and tidying!

write_csv(lipidata, "~/SDS264_S26/Project/MP1/lipidframe.csv")

title: PubMed Text Analysis
text:
The prevalent undergoing research done in the Biology Department at St. Olaf specifically on Lipid Droplet in Tetrahymena thermophila has inspired the initiation of this project. Here I propose to investigate literature on the general topic of “Lipid Droplet” or LD, which is known to be an important biomarker in many pathological diseases. Given their importance across many biological systems and the increasing amount of research tied to LDs, it is valuable and highly interesting for me to examine how research on lipid droplets has evolved over time and how knowledge within this field is being generated.
Inspired from “Literature Based Discovery” or LBD and the fact that researchers are always trying to be on top of new discoveries or new publications, I would like to see and uncover lipid droplet research trends, and other things associated with it (e.g. Who (is/are) the currently most active researchers for LDs research based on this data set? What are the most frequent keywords appearing when we are exploring this particular research field?). Scientific literature is growing at an exponential rate causing researchers to become increasingly specialized, and making it difficult for researchers to stay current in even their narrow discipline. There is too much information for anyone to read, much less understand. This overwhelming volume of publications has led to specialized, non-interacting literature, creating isolated knowledge in which discoveries in one specific area are not known by people who are doing research by the field next door. LBD has a goal of increasing interdisciplinary through prompt and more accessible information sharing, using the resources that are available out there without us realising it in order to increase the impact of scientific literature even further. LBD is then a necessary tool for facilitating research. LBD has led to countless discovery proposals ranging from treatments for cataracts, multiple sclerosis, and Parkinson’s Disease, to understanding and discovering new health benefits of curcumin, and potential treatments for cancer.
In this data science project, I would not be carrying any sophisticated scientific analysis on LDs literature due to constraint in time and whatnot. However, I would have this personal motivation at the back of my head as I am going through this project, as something that I could potentially revisit for a larger project with more well-founded, clear, and impactful goals towards scientific discoveries and literacy. Thus, this project will be more focused in data exploratory and finding out what one can do with a compilation of scientific literature made into a data set.

title: PubMed Text Analysis
text:
The prevalent undergoing research done in the Biology Department at St. Olaf specifically on Lipid Droplet in Tetrahymena thermophila has inspired the initiation of this project. Here I propose to investigate literature on the general topic of “Lipid Droplet” or LD, which is known to be an important biomarker in many pathological diseases. What are the most frequent keywords appearing when we are exploring this particular research field?). Scientific literature is growing at an exponential rate causing researchers to become increasingly specialized, and making it difficult for researchers to stay current in even their narrow discipline. There is too much information for anyone to read, much less understand. This overwhelming volume of publications has led to specialized, non-interacting literature, creating isolated knowledge in which discoveries in one specific area are not known by people who are doing research by the field next door. In this data science project, I would not be carrying any sophisticated scientific analysis on LDs literature due to constraint in time and whatnot. However, I would have this personal motivation at the back of my head as I am going through this project, as something that I could potentially revisit for a larger project with more well-founded, clear, and impactful goals towards scientific discoveries and literacy. Thus, this project will be more focused in data exploratory and finding out what one can do with a compilation of scientific literature made into a data set.

title: PubMed Text Analysis
text:
Webscraping and Data Production
PubMed is an openly accessible, free database which includes primarily the database of references and abstracts on life sciences and biomedical topics. Owing to that, I was able to webscrape their rich literary archives, specifically on Lipid Droplet. In order to do that, I used an automated protocol to specifically scrape PubMed database created by the user erilu on Github. The protocol used python to automate the construction of the URLs associated with webscraping, execution of the “search” and “fetch” commands, and parse each part of the abstract (Authors, Journal, Date of publication, etc.) into a data file for further reproducible analysis. As a result from this programming script, each abstract from each scientific article can be analyzed to quickly extract numerical data or quantitative results.
The python script that was run in R and established my data sets (abstracts.csv and abstracts_lipid.csv) can be found in my MP1 Github repository here.

title: PubMed Text Analysis
text:
Data Wrangling & Tidying
Following a good data science workflow methodology, where one is supposed to do data wrangling in another document and then plugging it back in into a separate document designated for data analysis, the complete data wrangling and tidying can be found in my MP1 Github repository here. This document also contains my main usage of str_functions and regular expressions.

title: PubMed Text Analysis
text:
Text Analysis
When I first examined the fresh data set generated by the python script, I was highly curious who is the most published author in this PubMed data set, with the topic of Lipid Droplet. So, my text analysis started modest and simple, let’s see who is the major “First Author”, which is most of the times regarded as the most significant intellectual, experimental, and writing contributor to a research paper. When a paper is cited, that first author is usually who will be referenced to, both through bibliography or through word of mouth. Let’s see who is the major “First Author” in this data set.


# A tibble: 5 × 2
  FA          n
  <chr>   <int>
1 Wang Y     56
2 Zhang Y    56
3 Li Y       55
4 Li X       38
5 Liu Y      34




# A tibble: 239 × 2
   FA      FA_institution                                                       
   <chr>   <chr>                                                                
 1 Li Y    "Department of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Wuhan Fourth Hospital." 
 2 Wang Y  "Department of Rehabilitation, The Fourth Clinical Medical College o…
 3 Zhang Y "Department of Animal Science, College of Agriculture, Yanbian Unive…
 4 Zhang Y "Department of Anesthesiology, Wuhan University, Renmin Hospital, Wu…
 5 Li Y    "Shanghai Key Laboratory of Orthopedic Implants, Department of Ortho…
 6 Li Y    "Key Laboratory of Carbohydrate Chemistry and Biotechnology, Ministr…
 7 Li X    "Key Laboratory of Animal Genetics, Breeding and Reproduction in the…
 8 Wang Y  "College of Animal & Veterinary Sciences, Southwest Minzu University…
 9 Liu Y   "Shaanxi Province Key Laboratory of Bioresources, QinLing-Bashan Mou…
10 Wang Y  "Department of Clinical Pharmacology, College of Pharmacy, Dalian Me…
# ℹ 229 more rows


Turns out that many of them are all very common names, and after observing, we can almost conclusively say that each individual there is different, and they come from different institutions. So now, I check for a combination of author-institution.
Good enough for me! Even though I believe we missed a couple authors here and there, because of the enormous inconsistency in how the institution was reported in literature publications, we received some good results, and we can focus on this for our text analysis.
Note: The behind-the-scene codes can be found in my Github repository.


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`









Joining with `by = join_by(word)`









Joining with `by = join_by(word)`









Simple one-word analysis might be interesting in the beginning, but we can quickly realise that it is too hard to infer anything from it. Therefore, what we will do next is bigram or two-words analysis.


# A tibble: 254,566 × 2
   bigram                    n
   <chr>                 <int>
 1 adipose tissue         1328
 2 endoplasmic reticulum   914
 3 oxidative stress        899
 4 signaling pathway       561
 5 hepatic steatosis       556
 6 body weight             502
 7 binding protein         486
 8 insulin resistance      453
 9 reactive oxygen         446
10 oxygen species          442
# ℹ 254,556 more rows


# A tibble: 173,965 × 2
   bigram                                n
   <chr>                             <int>
 1 reactive oxygen species             441
 2 peroxisome proliferator activated   414
 3 proliferator activated receptor     408
 4 endoplasmic reticulum er            392
 5 oxygen species ros                  244
 6 low density lipoprotein             243
 7 activated protein kinase            192
 8 brown adipose tissue                166
 9 regulatory element binding          163
10 sterol regulatory element           161
# ℹ 173,955 more rows


# A tibble: 1,593 × 2
   bigram                 n
   <chr>              <int>
 1 clo pxgs              11
 2 ps nps                10
 3 β casein              10
 4 mfgm glycoproteins     9
 5 rna seq                6
 6 ga ch                  5
 7 marine fish            5
 8 mrna isoforms          5
 9 tpa cyp                5
10 tpa trdn               5
# ℹ 1,583 more rows


# A tibble: 768 × 2
   bigram                            n
   <chr>                         <int>
 1 a2 β casein                       4
 2 β carboline alkaloid              3
 3 adipose tissue browning           2
 4 base cross linking                2
 5 bilayer contact angle             2
 6 branchial epithelial liftings     2
 7 camellia oil body                 2
 8 casein genotype a1a1              2
 9 casein milk variant               2
10 cc bonds equalled                 2
# ℹ 758 more rows


We have got the relevant two-words and three-words combination. Now, it is time to visualise these combinations using a network graph.


















Another interesting analysis we can do is finding tf-idf statistics. It is highly useful in the application of search engines, as this is the statistics behind ranking document in relevance to a query. Through this statistics, we can identify the most descriptive words in a document. In this case, I would try to find the important words for the content of Top 5 Journal Publishers for LD research. In this case, tf-idf will find the important words of each journal by decreasing the weight for commonly used words and increasing the weight for words that are not used very much in a collection of articles We want to find words that may or may not define one journal as opposed to others.


# A tibble: 5 × 2
  j_name          freq
  <chr>          <int>
1 Int J Mol Sci.   279
2 Food Chem.       160
3 Sci Rep.         128
4 Food Res Int.    124
5 Nat Commun.      112


We have the Top 5 journals listed above, some of them are specialising in food science, some are in molecular science, and the rest are in general natural sciences, including physics, chemistry, earth sciences, medicine, and biology.

title: PubMed Text Analysis
text:
Results
Interestingly, there are many instances where word combinations such as ‘remain elusive’, ‘remain poorly understood’, ‘remain unclear’ score pretty high in terms of the number of occurrences. As this was not the kind of term combination I was looking for, coming across this phrase across different categories was definitely unexpected yet evoking another realisation that there are still many things we do not not about the mechanisms or LDs despite its important function and despite the whopping number of scientific articles available for it. This makes text analysis such as this even more important, because only through this way we could provide communication bridges between authors investigating the same niche problem, which otherwise would be impossible due the amount of articles available online. Scientists could definitely meet in conferences, but distance and cost aspects are constraints that sometimes come in between in this valuable knowledge sharing.
When looking at the list of common two or three words combination, I saw many combinations that include “strange”, “unfamiliar”, and “improper” words, such as “clo pxgs” and “tpa cyp”. While at first they might seem abstract and irrelevant for people outside of the field, I reckon how they might be a name that scientists created for protein or any important part of the research methodology. Turns out, “clo pxgs” is the name of the protein sitting outside of the LD surface and “tpa cyp” is a name of fluorescent dye used for tracking LDs in research. Even though these terms feel distant at first and it is hard to understand directly what and why it is correlated with LD, with some quick google search or filtering in my data set, I was able to know what kind of LD research these keywords are associated with. I understand as well that these primary analysis might not be the best for people outside of the science field and that is why the goal of this kind of text analysis is very specific, which is to unpack various LD research findings.

title: PubMed Text Analysis
text:
Conclusion and Future Projects
There are many limitations in this project and the process I undertook, which some of them I have conveyed throughout my narrative. The data set could have been cleaned even further and there are many string elements that could be extracted and explored. For example, I did not play that much with author information, which would provide valuable information about the top leading departments and institutiona for LD research. Therefore, a bigger and more extensive analysis on scientific literature compilation is definitely possible. Another possibility is also to extract full article texts, to do a more full-on sentence by sentence investigation. Though I believe only extracting abstracts might be more suitable for word to word analysis, because the point of abstract to give brief summary using important keywords, so we should be effectively getting good results from there. Limiting only to “most popular papers” on certain topic, however, should be avoided because it defies the purpose of exploring each unique and distinct crevasses of observations.

title: Choropleth Map Project
text:
Numeric variable (static plot):









Numeric variable (interactive plot):






Categorical variable (static plot):









Categorical variable (interactive plot):






I definitely prefer the interactive plot. Even with making this conclusive closing, I felt that the interactive plot is very helpful with navigating the different states and also helped me to quickly identify which state is which. In places where gay supports are low (denoted by red), the prolife support is really high (denoted by yellow/brighter color). For example, states like Utah scores 61 in prolife support index and has low gay rights support.

title: Spring Break Thoughts
text:
It is halfway through spring break. The Hill is obviously quiet and deserted; I mentioned that despite how much I hate the myriad of ways that it could be discouragingly overwhelming. This place has its magical ways to withstand the crowds, frame the chaos, gather and hold people together. I have lost count of the number of times I curse Stav during dinner time rush, wishing that they built two dining spaces like that one college - that I strangely can’t remember the name? - just across the river.
Simply put, for almost every single break where I had to stay on the Hill, I observed and told myself how this place is meant to be lively, full of people, and give you that feeling of warmth- kind of juxtaposing its famous first impression of having that mysterious… dark academia vibes owing to its architecture. Now, having read that sentence, you might think that I am over-romanticising or over-describing this place (one-year-ago-myself would probably cringe over this description), but I am starting to get the distinctive vibe this place gives off after having visited other “American colleges”. There is a fixed, set atmosphere and ambience, notoriously due to its Norwegian and Church inspired architecture style that you probably could not find anywhere else, considering its status as a small private liberal art college in the midwest (or more like, in the middle of nowhere…).
Like, it is easily distinguishable - imagine yourself being in a geoguessr game and you are suddenly being dropped at any point within St. Olaf complex - you would KNOW instantly, that you are here, on the Hill. I’m not joking - and I thought that would not be the case with other American colleges, and yes, I am biased, but you just have got to experience this place firsthand. At first, the architecture could feel oddly strange - but then you kind of realise that you could not really find this anywhere else. Other American colleges look nothing like this place, and that was my first surprise as an international student who had never visited or toured other colleges before coming here.
Coming back to that one college across the river, yes, they do have two smaller dining spaces, which are way less overwhelming and overstimulating and whatnot, but during my first time visiting there - with crazily high expectations - my jaw dropped, perplexed rather than astonished, “Is this it?”. My eyes were hovering everywhere, trying to search for corners and sights of wonders or even peculiarities that could satisfy my curiosity with this place (hint: I never did). I remember vividly when I was eating- which some Oles described the food to be better (just because of their soft-serve machine, which I did not even bother to try) - my mind replayed some familiar scenes from when I was sitting in a random shopping mall food court back home. Like, I could be dropped on this place in a geoguessr game, and I would guess, “This could be either a food court somewhere in TP or Ciputra World” (IYKYK). If there is one thing that I saw and thought “damn, St. Olaf could never”, is the fact that they have three waffle machines (we have two) - but that lasted a good two seconds and I moved on, so anyway…
But with St. Olaf’s Stav - despite how overwhelming it is - I recalled how I have always been in awe every time I stepped inside, particularly after stepping outside of the buffet area to the actual dining hall. Special mention especially when it is in the morning and during golden hour time. It is majestic - I have to acknowledge that, and my mind screams “Oh my God” every time I have to find somewhere to sit, both because (guess what?!) I could probably not find anywhere to sit since it is so crowded, but also because, oh my God! It is so grand, majestic, the huge ass windows give me reasons and hopes to live (OK, that’s kind of dramatic), and the damn tall ceilings set the bar high for a college dining hall - it is an experience of just coming in there.
Coming from someone introverted, mediocrely social and frequently wishes that we had fewer students on campus; therefore, everywhere does not always have to be packed with everyone, every time - I hate to admit this, but St. Olaf is better off with these crazy, chaotic good people. It is hard to picture St. Olaf without this exact community of people - with both the diversity and shared values that everyone contributes to the table.
The moral of the story is, “feeling overwhelmed” tells me how fortunate I am to be in this busy and chaotic yet supportive environment. There is always something happening - whether you are interested or not, it is your choice. There is always a place to be - whether this is the kind of student lifestyle you aim for, it is your choice. There is always someone to go to - whether you realise it or not, it is your choice. Now, the last one is indescribably tricky - and as an international freshman myself, I am still working towards reaching its highest potential.
I am not there yet, but I am slowly realising how the game is played. The truth is, and it is kind of paradoxical, you ought not to keep exposing yourself to a variety of options because that is not how life works. Sometimes, you have to limit the options you can choose while you can - before the real world decides it for you. What I am trying to say here is: challenge and experiment with yourself. We may think the fact that we have so many options to choose - is overwhelming. “Wow! I have a variety of options to choose from - I can’t choose!” This is exactly what we take for granted! If you keep picking options and constantly tailoring your day only for the sense of comfortability and familiarity, will you ever find something new and wonderful? (hint, if you are following my story: what did I find in the dining hall across the river?)
Face and overcome the overwhelming inherent nature of any instances you are exposed to. Limited options, chaos, disorder, rush hours - these will lead you to quick decision-making. Fight the overwhelming feeling with sharp decisiveness. This overwhelming feeling, therefore, should not be something that stops or discourages you. What is it then? I am also still exploring it - of course I have not fully mastered it yet. I do have a belief that it is a sign that you are experiencing something new; you are outside your comfort zone, and isn’t that enough to be grateful for? For I argue that many people wish they could shift away from beyond their box. Trust me on that. The next time you feel overwhelmed, step in and face it; you shall find wonders inside, and you might surprise yourself (hint: huge windows and tall ceilings!). Just like my love-hate relationship with Stav, no matter how hard I think about it, and I do not know about you, but: I do not want an ordinary, boring looking dining hall that looks just like a common food court I could easily find back home. Surprise me with chaotic experiences (and beautiful windows and ceilings)! Oh, did I mention that crazy time when the XC table clapped on me because someone dropped their glass from the second floor exactly when I was going downstairs? Anyway -
St. Olaf is reminding me that the world keeps turning, and it is a good thing and I am in the right place.
Written by an international Ole, Class of 2028. She’s a shy, secret hype fan of St. Olaf that would not confess - do not tell anyone.
@ Carleton College, jokes aside, I do love the fact that you have pesto, though, which I ought to try the next time I come there :)

title: Let’s Reimagine Democracy Globally
text:
You most likely do not remember this exact feeling, or you may think this younger version of yourself is just being dramatic, but attending both the Introduction to Public Policy course and the Imagining Democracy Globally seminar class was definitely overwhelming. You never thought about the possibility of learning politics and exploring an abundance of ideas and opinions freely, expressively. Your current self was struggling to find her voice and reflect on this matter, as she has never engaged in any political discourse meaningfully, and discussions surrounding politics and its ideas were “better kept personal”.
In case you are wondering, it all began with the simple yet complex question of “Who decides and how?”. Thank her for courageously taking these unfamiliar and uncomfortable courses as it became a stepping stone in learning the term of “effective citizenship”. While your public policy introduction course offers a definite answer to the initial question and you learnt all of the complexities and the intricacies behind the mechanics policymakers are grappling with, your democracy course provides a different perspective by approaching political ideas beyond mere governance structures. The course took you profoundly far from your original question by imagining what is outside the conventional boundaries of our current political system - beyond mere policy debates and elections - and considering the wider purpose of why we are following this pattern of governance in the first place. A new question proliferates - with a heavy consideration of the digital age - on what could democracy possibly look like when we emphasise the fact that it is a “practice”, an ongoing project that requires the engagement and commitment of all individuals, rather than labeling it as a set of institutions or formal processes.
The word democracy used to be a jargon that you never fully understood. However, you started to understand its values and meaning through the course materials. Some of your favourite materials include the early TEDTalks from Brett Hennig, asking what happens if we replace politicians with random people and Shari Davis’s explanation on Participatory Budgeting. Both criticised how our democracy is broken and does not function the way it should, as nowadays, elections resemble merely aristocratic devices. They proposed different kinds of solutions but similar conclusions to this problem: increasing ordinary citizens’ participation in the governing body while emphasising demographic profile diversity. I vividly remember how this strangely coincided with some number of people randomly coming up to me, mentioning that they found out Indonesia was the third largest democracy in the world and “how cool that is”. Considering these course materials, particularly Schmitter and Karl’s “What Democracy Is… and Is Not”, I wondered why this fancy title is just a complete fallacy into what S&K described as “electoralism”, where the mere existence of elections is adequate for democracy. For sure, Indonesia has a great number of electorates and the 82.39% voter turnout ranks Indonesia well above the global average of 65.6% (Westminster Foundation for Democracy 2024), but while I would gladly remind you how democracy severely backslides in the 2024 election (I think you know better how Prabowo’s administration unfolded at the end), I want to discuss a more persistent issue drawn from the intersection of digital era and weak citizenship that does not reflect our voting turnout.
Right now, I can discern how the presence of technology and social media dangerously eats away at citizenship and democratic institutions. While I agree that modern media acts as a catalyst and tool to encourage individuals to articulate their opinions, participate in the public sphere, and challenge state and traditional narratives by the expansion of the public sphere, you must remember what Nanjala Nyabola critically mentioned in her book Digital Democracy, Analogue Politics. She explained how social media platforms we are using were not developed with partly free countries such as Kenya in mind, therefore, we should be wise and careful in implementing its practice into local contexts. Likewise, these technologies were never made for the sake of “digital commons”, let alone to promote social welfare or empower civic participation. Ironically, these companies have profit motives, business models, data-gathering practices, process or procedural opacity and power that do not in any way promote democracy. In the latest election campaign, I witnessed a myriad of buzzers from representatives of political parties or supporters of candidates using Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, WhatsApp, YouTube, and similar applications to exert influence. These buzzers push certain agendas and determine the major campaign narratives. Artificial intelligence and algorithms decide what information is presented to the public through filtering mechanisms where opinions can continue to polarise and radicalise - and no one could be held accountable. There is still a big tendency amongst Indonesians to vote for candidates because of certain race, religion, ethnicity, and familiarity. Voter turn out is high, but can it actually be referred to as “effective citizenship”?
Eventually going and giving back to the community that raised who I am today has been a firm goal that keeps me motivated today. Wherever you are right now in 2045, this year, you are celebrating Indonesia’s 100th year of independence. Whether you would have moved back to Indonesia at this point, this is the time testament where I wish to see a free and independent public sphere where Indonesians have a robust structured discourse within civil society that highly esteemed effective rational-critical debate, therefore resulting in effective resolution of political disputes and genuine democratic engagement. I learned the hard way that technology will inevitably keep developing to become more sophisticated than ever. Whether you like it or not, it is indeed a paradox of development that you would constantly grapple with whatever field you are working in right now.
On the trajectory towards 2045, I argue that resistance against big social media platforms must emerge now. These corporations only allow people to receive comfortable information and reinforce existing tendencies toward confirmation bias. With Indonesia’s persistent problem regarding literacy, I worry that my aspiration is only wishful thinking which means that by 2045, despite celebrating the country’s milestone, its citizens will continue to be dictated and controlled with instant and most readily available information in online platforms, further reinforcing a general public who is uninterested in being educated and understanding opposing viewpoints.
While I, realistically, do not foresee you being a policy-maker passing bills in the matter of transforming the Indonesian education curriculum, specifically establishing a civic education that goes beyond strengthening national identity but also penetrating democracy values, you ought to remember one thing: that your younger self has long been passionate about the transforming impact of education. I hope that amidst all of your ambitious science-related endeavours, you would spare a meaningful amount of time and energy to contribute to Indonesia’s education sector in any possible way. You have realised for a long time how democracy can only work if there is an informed citizenry. Advocating for technological literacy or being involved in research to provide policy recommendations could be some ways to fight against artificial intelligence and algorithm infestation. Over the years, I believe that you, by now, will have shaped a confident and firm belief in media literacy and civic responsibility and why they matter for democracy, which will naturally guide your way into advocacy or policy research.
At the end, politics was never supposed to be something personal. Calhoun, through his book “Civil Society and the Public Sphere”, mentioned that being a part of the civil society means coordinating our common affairs together through collective discourse, which transcends the private interests of each other. We must debate, share our piece of cultural creativity and diversity to benefit the larger public concern dialogue. One crucial key is therefore courage. Deeply inspired by Wangari Maathai’s work through the Green Belt movement, she fundamentally educated Kenyan women in particular regarding their rights to speak up and how to stand up for what they believe in despite intimidation.
Every human being deserves to amplify their voices and opinions. It is our responsibility to guide the future generation into meaningfully participating in public discourse. The standard of the common idea of democracy has to be raised. We should present what democracy could look like, rather than what it is. Democracy can only happen when people find their voices, opinions, and values respected and heard. Democracy does not happen just because “people participate”, which, most of the time, is just limited to elections and voting. Democracy on a personal level happens when everyone’s mind is free and has the capability to see the truth. Democracy happens when people stand up for things they believe in. This also means that real democracy can only happen when there are efforts to advance the thinking capability of a nation, encouraging people to realise what their rights are. To merely participate in democracy is easier than actively engaging in its deeper responsibilities: understanding the complexities of various issues, mobilising and empowering communities, and, importantly, holding leaders accountable. Actions toward effective citizenship demand sustained effort. You must light and pass that torch of courage and determination in order for this to happen.

title: About
text:
I am a member of the Lipid Lab and we are currently researching the mechanisms of lipid droplet synthesis and degradation in Tetrahymena thermophila! I am an internationally certified Supplemental Instruction leader for introductory chemistry courses, namely CHEM 122: Introductory Chemistry and CHEM 126: Energies and Rates of Chemical Reaction.










Me in the lab!
